content.text: define how long observability data — traces, logs, and metrics — must be stored, in what form, and under what access controls, to satisfy regulatory obligations. For agentic AI systems, compliance retention intersects two distinct domains: the standard software observability obligations (SOC 2, ISO 27001) that apply to any production system, and AI-specific obligations that arise when agents handle personal data, health information, financial decisions, or other regulated content. A retention policy specifies minimum storage duration, required data format (raw vs. redacted), immutability requirements, geographic constraints, and deletion procedures for each class of observability data.

Regulators and auditors increasingly treat AI system logs as first-class evidence. HIPAA audit controls require six years of access log retention; SOX requires seven years of records for financial decision systems; GDPR imposes conflicting obligations — retain for legal purposes but delete on user request. Failing to retain required records can result in regulatory penalties and failed audits; retaining data longer than permitted increases breach exposure and legal liability. Agentic AI traces are particularly sensitive because they may contain verbatim user prompts, model responses, and intermediate reasoning steps that constitute personal data under GDPR or protected health information under HIPAA. Without explicit, compliant retention policies, teams default to keeping everything forever — accumulating both risk and cost.
Demonstrable compliance during audits: timestamps prove traces were retained for the required minimum period.
Reduced breach exposure by deleting personal data from traces after the minimum required period.
Cost control through tiered storage: compliance archives move to cold storage (S3 Glacier) after hot retention expires.
Clear incident investigation scope: policies define exactly which traces are available for a given time range.
Support for GDPR right-to-erasure requests by tagging traces with user identity at ingest and enabling targeted deletion.
Long mandatory retention periods (6–7 years) create large compliance archives that must be maintained, secured, and eventually deleted correctly.
Conflicts between retention (keep for N years) and deletion (right to erasure) require pseudonymisation or selective attribute deletion rather than complete trace deletion.
Correct tagging at ingest requires application-layer knowledge of which regulations apply to a given trace — generic infrastructure cannot determine this automatically.
Immutable storage (WORM) prevents retroactive correction of incorrectly stored traces — errors in retention classification become permanent.
Geographic constraints (GDPR data residency) require separate storage infrastructure per region, increasing operational complexity.
— implemented as an OTel Collector processor — inspects span attributes to determine which regulatory regimes apply. Application code attaches
) based on the data types processed during the agent run. The classifier then routes the trace to the appropriate retention tier with the correct storage backend, encryption requirements, and TTL.

Each tier enforces its retention window through storage-layer lifecycle policies (S3 lifecycle rules, Glacier vault locks). When a trace reaches end-of-life, an automated deletion job removes it and logs the deletion event to an audit trail. For GDPR right-to-erasure requests, a separate deletion pipeline identifies all traces tagged with a specific user's pseudonym and either deletes the traces or strips the PII attributes while retaining the anonymised trace structure for the remainder of its compliance window.

attrs.alt: Trace retention compliance tiers diagram: hot storage for debugging, SOC2 one year, HIPAA six years, GDPR with erasure rights, financial seven years

content.text: The compliance classifier tags each trace at ingest with the applicable regulatory scope. Traces are then routed to the appropriate storage tier with its required retention duration, encryption, and immutability settings. A shared lifecycle management layer handles tier transitions, redaction on cold storage, and deletion on expiry.
Any agentic AI system operating under SOC 2, HIPAA, GDPR, SOX, FCA, or ISO 27001 requirements.
Systems where agent traces may contain personal data, PII, PHI, or other regulated information categories.
Organisations facing audits where log retention is an audit control (CC7.2 in SOC 2, §164.312(b) in HIPAA).
Multi-jurisdiction deployments where different data residency rules apply to different user populations.
Map each regulatory regime to a concrete retention duration, storage tier, encryption requirement, and deletion obligation. Document this as a data classification matrix.
Instrument agent application code to set compliance scope attributes on root spans before emitting to the collector.
Configure an OTel Collector routing connector to dispatch traces by compliance scope attribute to separate exporter pipelines.
Set up S3 lifecycle policies (or equivalent) to transition objects to Glacier after the hot-storage window and to delete objects after the full retention period.
For HIPAA and financial tiers, enable S3 Object Lock in WORM mode with the retention duration set to the regulatory minimum.
Build a GDPR deletion pipeline that accepts a user pseudonym, scans the trace index, and either deletes matching traces or strips PII attributes while retaining compliance metadata.
Emit a deletion audit event for every trace deletion, storing it in the compliance audit log with a longer retention than the deleted trace.

# Agent instrumentation: set compliance scope on root span
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent.service")

def process_patient_query(patient_id: str, query: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.session") as span:
        # Tag with applicable compliance scopes
        span.set_attribute("compliance.scope", "hipaa")
        span.set_attribute("compliance.region", "us-east-1")
        # Use pseudonymised ID, not the real patient ID
        span.set_attribute("user.pseudonym", pseudonymise(patient_id))
        span.set_attribute("agent.use_case", "patient_support")
        # Do NOT put PHI in span attributes — only in redacted logs
        return run_agent(query)

def pseudonymise(patient_id: str) -> str:
    import hmac, hashlib
    return hmac.new(PSEUDONYMISE_KEY, patient_id.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()[:16]

# OTel Collector: route by compliance scope
connectors:
  routing:
    default_pipelines: [traces/hot]
    error_mode: ignore
    table:
      - statement: route() where attributes["compliance.scope"] == "hipaa"
        pipelines: [traces/hipaa]
      - statement: route() where attributes["compliance.scope"] == "gdpr"
        pipelines: [traces/gdpr]
      - statement: route() where attributes["compliance.scope"] == "financial"
        pipelines: [traces/financial]

pipelines:
  traces/hot:
    receivers: [otlp]
    exporters: [tempo]
  traces/hipaa:
    receivers: [routing]
    processors: [transform/redact_phi]
    exporters: [s3/hipaa_worm]
  traces/gdpr:
    receivers: [routing]
    processors: [transform/pseudonymise]
    exporters: [s3/gdpr_eu]
  traces/financial:
    receivers: [routing]
    exporters: [s3/financial_worm]
A digital health platform deploys an AI symptom-checker agent that processes patient queries. Each agent session is tagged at the root span with

and the patient's pseudonymised ID. The OTel Collector routes these traces to an S3 bucket with Object Lock enabled in Governance mode for six years. Before writing to S3, a transform processor strips any span attributes that might contain PHI (raw query text, symptom descriptions) and replaces them with a redaction token. The original PHI is written to a separate, more tightly controlled audit log.

When a patient exercises their GDPR right to erasure, the deletion pipeline queries the trace index for all traces associated with the patient's pseudonym, strips the pseudonym attribute from those traces, and logs a deletion event. The anonymised trace structure remains for the HIPAA retention window; the personal identifier is gone. During a HIPAA audit, the team can demonstrate continuous trace retention for all agent sessions in scope, with no gaps in the six-year window.
Storing raw user prompts (which may contain PHI or PII) in trace span attributes without redaction, creating a compliance violation in the observability pipeline itself.
Using real user IDs instead of pseudonyms in traces, making GDPR right-to-erasure impossible without deleting entire traces.
Setting retention to "indefinitely" to avoid the complexity of lifecycle policies — this accumulates deletion liability and storage cost.
Conflating hot-storage debugging retention (30–90 days) with compliance retention (1–7 years) — they require different storage tiers and cost structures.
Not retaining a deletion audit log, which is itself a compliance requirement: you must prove what was deleted and when.
Classify traces at the application layer — infrastructure cannot determine regulatory scope without business context baked into span attributes.
Use pseudonymous user identifiers in all traces; maintain the pseudonym-to-identity mapping in a separate, access-controlled store.
Apply attribute redaction in the OTel Collector pipeline before traces reach any storage backend, not retroactively.
Enable S3 Object Lock (WORM) for HIPAA and financial tiers — this provides tamper-evident immutability that auditors can verify.
Review retention durations with legal counsel annually — regulatory requirements change, and AI-specific guidance continues to evolve.
Test deletion pipelines regularly — run deletion dry-runs quarterly to verify they correctly identify and remove traces for a test pseudonym.

Compliance archives grow without bound until deletion policies engage. A system retaining traces for seven years with 6 million spans per hour will accumulate roughly 50 TB over the retention period. Lifecycle tier transitions (hot → IA → Glacier) reduce ongoing storage cost by an order of magnitude but introduce retrieval latency: restoring from Glacier for an audit can take 3–12 hours. Plan restoration timelines into audit response procedures. Index the archive by date and compliance scope to minimise the blast radius of retrieval requests.

GDPR erasure pipelines must complete within 30 days of request under GDPR Article 17. Design the erasure pipeline to operate in batch mode off the primary write path, processing erasure requests against the full archive. Use a pseudonym index (a separate database mapping pseudonym → trace object keys) to make erasure scans O(1) in the index and O(traces per user) in storage, rather than scanning the entire archive.
Governance or Compliance mode per tier
Very high SaaS cost at volume

Compliance archives are high-value targets for attackers and insider threats because they contain historical records of all agent behaviour. Enforce least-privilege access: only the audit response process should have read access to compliance-tier storage, and no human should have write access. Use IAM conditions to restrict S3 Object Lock bypass to a break-glass role requiring multi-party approval. Enable S3 server-side encryption with customer-managed KMS keys; rotate keys annually.

Reliability for compliance archives means detecting and alerting on gaps in ingestion, not just outages. A missing 30 minutes of traces in a HIPAA system during an audit window can be treated as a control failure. Use a trace sequence ID or monotonic counter on root spans to detect gaps: a Prometheus alert on large jumps in the sequence metric signals a retention gap before it becomes an audit finding.
Handling conflicts between retention and deletion obligations

The most challenging compliance scenario for agentic AI traces is when two obligations collide: a regulation requires you to retain records for six years (HIPAA), while another gives users the right to have their data deleted within 30 days (GDPR). These obligations apply simultaneously when an EU patient uses an AI health assistant. The standard resolution is

: you retain the trace structure and timing information (required for the audit control) but strip or replace the personal identifier, making the trace no longer directly linkable to an identifiable individual under GDPR. This satisfies the "no longer personal data" exemption while preserving the audit evidence.

Document this resolution in your Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) under GDPR Article 30, explicitly noting that pseudonymised traces retained for HIPAA compliance are no longer considered personal data after erasure of the pseudonym mapping. Have legal counsel review this position, as data protection authorities in different EU member states may interpret this differently.
A compliance audit will typically request evidence that specific traces exist for a defined time window. Prepare for this by maintaining a

— a separate, fast-query database that records the trace ID, compliance scope, storage location, retention expiry date, and ingestion timestamp for every trace in the compliance archive. This index lets you respond to audit requests with sub-minute retrieval of trace locations without scanning the entire archive. The index itself must be retained at least as long as the traces it describes.

# Compliance trace index entry (stored in DynamoDB or similar)
class ComplianceTraceRecord:
    trace_id: str              # OTel trace ID
    compliance_scope: str      # "hipaa", "gdpr", "financial"
    user_pseudonym: str        # for GDPR erasure lookups
    ingested_at: datetime      # ISO 8601
    retention_expires_at: datetime  # ingested_at + required_period
    storage_uri: str           # s3://bucket/prefix/trace_id.json.gz
    redacted: bool             # True if PII attributes stripped
    deleted: bool              # True if erasure applied
    deletion_logged_at: Optional[datetime]

# Audit response: retrieve all HIPAA traces for a date range
def get_compliance_traces(scope: str, start: datetime, end: datetime):
    return index.query(
        KeyConditionExpression=Key('compliance_scope').eq(scope) &
                              Key('ingested_at').between(start.isoformat(), end.isoformat())
    )
6 years from creation or last use
Compliance retention is distinct from operational retention — they have different durations, storage tiers, immutability requirements, and cost structures.
Classify traces at the application layer by setting compliance scope attributes on root spans; infrastructure cannot determine regulatory scope automatically.
Pseudonymise user identifiers in traces from the start — retrofitting pseudonymisation is prohibitively expensive and may be impossible on immutable archives.
Redact PHI and PII attributes in the OTel Collector pipeline before any storage backend receives the trace.
Use S3 Object Lock WORM mode for HIPAA and financial tiers; deletion is a compliance risk that immutability prevents.
Build a GDPR erasure pipeline before you need it — responding to erasure requests in 30 days requires index-based lookup, not full archive scans.
Retain the deletion audit log longer than the data it describes — you must prove what was deleted and when, not just that it was deleted.
